Cost-Effectiveness of SGLT2 Inhibitors vs. Placebo for Heart Failure Prevention in Type 2 Diabetes with Established ASCVD

A Markov model with deterministic and probabilistic sensitivity analysis

Author

Said Adjao

Published

June 27, 2026

title: “Cost-Effectiveness of SGLT2 Inhibitors vs. Placebo for Heart Failure Prevention in Type 2 Diabetes with Established ASCVD” subtitle: “A Markov model with deterministic and probabilistic sensitivity analysis” author: “Said Adjao” date: today format: html: toc: true number-sections: true embed-resources: true execute: echo: false warning: false message: false

1 Summary

This analysis evaluates the cost-effectiveness of SGLT2 inhibitors versus placebo for the prevention of heart failure hospitalization in adults with type 2 diabetes and established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD), using a Markov state-transition model. The treatment effect — a hazard ratio of 0.70 for HF hospitalization — was drawn from an independent meta-analysis of four cardiovascular outcome trials.

At a list price of $6,000/year, the base-case incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (ICER) was $275,688 per QALY, exceeding the US willingness-to-pay threshold of $150,000/QALY. The conclusion was robust to uncertainty in the treatment effect but highly sensitive to drug price.

2 Background

Heart failure is a frequent, costly complication of type 2 diabetes. SGLT2 inhibitors reduce HF hospitalization, but their value depends on whether the clinical benefit justifies the cost — the central question for payers and health technology assessment. This analysis links a meta-analytic estimate of effectiveness to an economic model to estimate cost per QALY in a defined high-risk population.

3 Methods

3.1 Model structure

A three-state Markov model (Stable; Post-HF hospitalization; Dead) was run over 20 annual cycles with 3% discounting of costs and QALYs. Patients begin Stable and may transition to Post-HF (a costed event), to Dead (absorbing), or remain. The treatment effect acts solely by reducing the probability of HF hospitalization (baseline rate × HR 0.70); all other parameters are identical between arms.

3.2 Parameters

Base-case model inputs (illustrative values).
Parameter Value Source
HF hospitalization rate (placebo, annual) 0.040 Trial placebo arms
Hazard ratio (SGLT2i vs placebo) 0.70 (95% CI 0.62–0.78) Meta-analysis (4 RCTs)
Background mortality (Stable) 0.020 Life tables
Mortality (Post-HF) 0.060 Post-HF literature
Drug cost (annual) $6,000 Drug pricing (list)
HF hospitalization cost $13,000 Costing study
Utility: Stable 0.85 Utility study
Utility: Post-HF 0.70 Utility study
Discount rate 3% Convention
Time horizon 20 years —

Effectiveness was pooled on the log scale (random-effects); costs and utilities were sourced from standard references. Note that values here are illustrative; a definitive analysis would cite a specific source for each.

4 Results

4.1 Base case

Base-case costs and QALYs per patient (discounted).
Arm Cost QALYs
Placebo $ 30,900 10.291
SGLT2i \(106,702 | 10.566| |Incremental |\) 75,801 0.275

The base-case ICER was $275,688 per QALY — above the US threshold, indicating SGLT2i is not cost-effective at list price under these assumptions.

4.2 One-way sensitivity: treatment effect

ICER across the 95% CI of the treatment effect.
Scenario ICER
Optimistic (HR 0.62) $214,254
Base case (HR 0.70) $275,688
Pessimistic (HR 0.78) $381,822

Across the full confidence interval of the treatment effect, the ICER remained above $150,000/QALY. The conclusion is therefore robust to uncertainty in effectiveness — the result is driven by other parameters, principally drug cost.

4.3 One-way sensitivity: drug price (threshold analysis)

SGLT2i reaches the $150,000/QALY threshold at an annual price of approximately $3,301. Because real-world net prices (after rebates) often fall near this range, list-price and net-price analyses can reach opposite conclusions — explaining the divergence between this model and favorable real-world evaluations.

4.4 Probabilistic sensitivity analysis

Accounting for all parameter uncertainty simultaneously, SGLT2i had an approximately 6% probability of being cost-effective at $150,000/QALY at list price. The acceptability curve crosses 50% near the base-case ICER, confirming internal consistency.

5 Limitations

This is a simplified three-state model with illustrative inputs; a definitive analysis would source and cite each parameter, model recurrent-event costs explicitly, and extend the survival structure. The base-case verdict is highly sensitive to drug price, and list-price assumptions differ materially from negotiated net prices. Results should be interpreted as a demonstration of method rather than a definitive coverage recommendation.

6 Conclusion

SGLT2 inhibitors substantially reduce HF hospitalization (HR 0.70), but at list price the incremental cost per QALY exceeds US willingness-to-pay thresholds in this population. The conclusion is robust to uncertainty in the treatment effect and driven primarily by drug cost; the drug becomes cost-effective at an annual price near $3,301. This illustrates a core HEOR principle: clinical effectiveness does not guarantee cost-effectiveness.
